title: This Teacher Built a Life-Size ENIAC Replica With His Students—And It's Awesome
summary:
Tom Burick has always been a builder. He's designed robots, constructed a vintage teardrop trailer, and now, as a technology instructor at PS Academy in Gilbert, Arizona, he's led his students in building a full-scale replica of one of the most important computers in history: ENIAC.

ENIAC, short for Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer, was one of the world's first programmable electronic computers. When it was built in the 1940s, it was about a thousand times faster than any other machine. The original ENIAC filled an entire room, weighed 30 tons, and used over 17,000 vacuum tubes. Burick's replica, built for the 80th anniversary of ENIAC's construction, precisely matches what the original computer looked like before it was disassembled in the 1950s.

But this project is about more than just history. Burick teaches at a middle and high school for students with autism and other specialized learning needs. He himself has dyscalculia, a learning difference that makes traditional mathematics challenging. "People tell you what it takes, but they never tell you what it gives," Burick says. For him, dyscalculia has been a superpower, giving him strong 3D spatial reasoning. "I have this CAD program that runs in my head 24 hours a day," he explains.

Burick wants his students to see their own neurodivergence as a strength. Through projects like the ENIAC replica, they can leverage traits like hyperfocus and the ability to precisely repeat tasks. "I didn't want my technology program to feel like craft hour," he says.

Before teaching, Burick owned a robotics company called White Box Robotics, which produced the 914 PC-Bot—a modular robot platform that sold about 200 units in 17 countries. But the 2008 financial crisis forced him to close the business. "I got to live my life's dream for 10 years," he says. Then he decided to pay forward the help he'd received as a young builder. "I had so many amazing people help me when I was young," he recalls. "I thought I need to pay that forward."
why_it_matters: Burick’s story shows that a learning difference like dyscalculia can be an asset rather than a limitation, and that hands-on projects can empower students with neurodivergence to build confidence and skills. For middle-schoolers, it challenges stereotypes about who can be a successful engineer or inventor.

keywords.explanation: One of the world's first programmable electronic computers, built in the 1940s.
Glass tubes that control electric current and were used as switches in early computers.
A learning difference that makes it hard to understand numbers and do math.
The idea that brain differences (like autism or dyscalculia) are normal and can be strengths.
The ability to concentrate intensely on a single task for a long time, often seen in people with certain neurodivergent traits.
A company founded by Tom Burick that made a modular robot called the 914 PC-Bot.

background_read: ENIAC (Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer) was built at the University of Pennsylvania in 1945. It filled a room 30 by 50 feet, weighed 30 tons, and used over 17,000 vacuum tubes. It could perform about 5,000 additions per second — a thousand times faster than earlier mechanical calculators.

Tom Burick's school, PS Academy in Gilbert, Arizona, is a K-12 school that specializes in educating students with autism and other unique learning needs. Burick himself has dyscalculia, a learning disorder that affects number sense and arithmetic. He used his strong 3D spatial reasoning — a common trait in some forms of neurodivergence — to design the replica without any blueprints.

Before teaching, Burick ran White Box Robotics, a company that produced the 914 PC‑Bot, a modular robot sold in 17 countries. The 2008 global financial crisis — a severe worldwide recession caused by the collapse of the housing market — forced him to close the business. He then turned to teaching to 'pay forward' the help he received as a young builder.

The concept of neurodiversity, which emerged in the late 1990s, argues that conditions like autism, dyslexia, and dyscalculia are natural variations in the human brain, not disorders to be cured. Burick's message to his students — that their neurodivergent traits can be strengths — reflects this growing movement.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Tom Burick is a builder who always takes on big projects.
└─ SPECIFIC: He and his students built a full-size replica of the ENIAC computer
ENIAC CONTEXT: Built in 1940s, filled a room, 30 tons, 17,000 vacuum tubes
PERSONAL ANGLE: Burick has dyscalculia — a learning difference that makes math hard
└─ HIS VIEW: Dyscalculia is a superpower that gives him 3D spatial reasoning ('CAD program in my head')
STUDENTS: School for students with autism; project uses hyperfocus and repeat-task abilities
└─ QUOTE: 'I didn't want my technology program to feel like craft hour'
BACKSTORY: Former owner of White Box Robotics (914 PC-Bot, sold in 17 countries)
└─ CLOSURE: 2008 financial crisis ended the business
CONCLUSION: He wants to pay forward the help he received as a young builder

perspectives.description: The project directly shows students with autism and other learning differences that their unique traits — hyperfocus, precise repetition, spatial thinking — are valuable in tech. Burick serves as a role model, proving that someone with dyscalculia can lead a complex engineering project.

Building a life-size ENIAC replica requires significant funds, space, and time. Critics might argue that such projects are too expensive for many schools, especially those serving under-resourced communities. The time spent on the replica could have been used for other educational activities.

The replica provides a tangible connection to the history of computing. Students learn not only about ENIAC’s engineering but also about the context of the 1940s — a time when computers were room-sized and women were often the operators. It’s a powerful hands-on lesson in technology history.

If Burick documents his process, other schools with specialized learning programs could replicate the project. This could inspire a broader movement to use large-scale historical builds as a teaching tool for neurodivergent students, potentially creating more inclusive pathways into STEM fields.
